title: Goat housing and bedding management
subtitle: Dry, draught-free housing, correct bedding depth, and a mucking-out schedule that keeps goats healthy.
excerpt: Goats need dry housing more urgently than most small livestock. They have little tolerance for sustained damp and will develop respiratory problems if ventilation is poor or bedding is left too long. This tutorial covers minimum space requirements, how to achieve good ventilation without draughts, and how often to muck out to maintain a healthy floor.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Goats (1991). https://www.gov.uk
British Goat Society, Goat Keeping Guidance. https://www.allgoats.com
NADAIS Goat Health Notes. https://www.nadis.org.uk

glossaryTerms.definition: A bedding management system in which fresh straw is added on top of existing bedding rather than removing it daily. Microbial activity in the lower layers generates heat that helps keep the floor warm through winter. Deep litter requires a full muck-out at least twice a year; without enough fresh material on top the system becomes wet and toxic.

The movement of fresh air through a building to remove moisture, ammonia, and pathogens. Good ventilation for goats means air movement at roof level without draughts at animal height. A ridge vent, open eaves, or a gap at the top of a wall all achieve this. Blocking all gaps to keep warmth in creates the damp conditions that cause pneumonia.

Ammonia gas produced by the breakdown of urine in wet bedding. At concentrations above 10 ppm it irritates the respiratory tract of goats and increases susceptibility to pneumonia. A sharp ammonia smell at animal height is the reliable indicator that bedding management or ventilation needs attention.

content.text: Goats are drier-climate animals. They handle cold far better than they handle damp. A well-ventilated shed that is consistently dry will keep goats healthier through winter than a warmer but humid one. The two things that cause the most housing-related problems are wet bedding and blocked ventilation. Both are easy to avoid.
Minimum space per animal

For housed dairy or meat goats, allow 1.5 square metres of lying space per animal as a working minimum. In practice 2 square metres per adult goat allows animals to lie fully stretched without touching each other and reduces competition for space at the hay rack. Kids need at least 0.5 square metres each. Overcrowding raises humidity, increases disease transmission, and causes bullying at feed stations.
Ventilation without draughts
Good
ventilation
means air moving at roof level, not at goat height. A ridge opening or open eaves allow moist air and
ammonia

to rise and escape. Solid walls to a height of 1.2 metres protect goats from ground-level draughts while the upper section remains open. Hessian sacking, temporary boarding, or mesh panels can close off the upper section during extreme cold spells; remove them during mild or wet weather. A draught test is simple: hold a piece of tissue at goat head height. It should hang still. At roof level it should move.
Bedding type and depth

Wheat straw is the standard bedding for housed goats. It is dry, absorbent, and comfortable to lie on. Barley straw works but is more prone to matting when wet. Shavings and sand are used in milking goat sheds where straw fragments in milk are a concern; both require a full muck-out more frequently than straw. Start the housed period with at least 15 cm of fresh straw. A goat lying on less than 10 cm of bedding over a concrete floor will suffer knee and hock injuries over a winter.
Muck-out frequency

There are two approaches. Daily partial mucking-out removes the wettest areas around water troughs and in the corners where goats urinate most. Add fresh straw on top of remaining dry bedding each day. Full muck-out happens when the base layer is compressed and the total depth falls below 15 cm, or when you can smell ammonia at floor level.
The
deep litter

approach skips the daily partial muck-out and relies on regular topping up. This works well in large dry sheds but poorly in small or poorly ventilated ones. If you use deep litter, plan a full muck-out at housing time in autumn and again at turnout in spring.

attrs.title: Ammonia is the warning sign
attrs.body: If you can smell ammonia when you walk into the shed, the bedding needs attention today. Ammonia at floor level affects kids especially quickly. Muck out the wet areas immediately and increase ventilation.

content.text: Daily checks while housed
Check all animals are standing and moving normally.
Check hay racks are full and every animal has access without being pushed away.
Check the water supply is clean and unfrozen.
Add fresh bedding to the wettest areas.
Note any animal that is off feed, coughing, or showing nasal discharge.
